title_en: Discourse on the Qi of the Five Viscera Patterned on the Seasons
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: In according the human body with the pattern of the four seasons and the five phases in treatment, what course follows the natural order and what course goes against it? I wish to hear the principles of gain and loss in this matter. Qibo replied: The five phases are metal, wood, water, fire, and earth. They alternately wax and wane in ascendancy, by which one may know death and life, determine success and failure, and fix the timing of the ebb and flow of qi in the five viscera — the periods of remission and exacerbation, and the times of death and life. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the whole of it. Qibo said: The liver governs spring; the foot jueyin and shaoyang channels govern its treatment; its days are jia and yi (the first two of the ten Heavenly Stems). The liver suffers from being drawn up tight, so one should promptly eat sweet foods to relax it. The heart governs summer; the hand shaoyin and taiyang channels govern its treatment; its days are bing and ding. The heart suffers from laxity, so one should promptly eat sour foods to constrict it. The spleen governs long summer; the foot taiyin and yangming channels govern its treatment; its days are wu and ji. The spleen suffers from dampness, so one should promptly eat sweet foods to relax it. The heart governs summer; the hand shaoyin and taiyang channels govern its treatment; its days are bing and ding. The heart suffers from laxity, so one should promptly eat sour foods to constrict it. The spleen governs long summer; the foot taiyin and yangming channels govern its treatment; its days are wu and ji. The spleen suffers from dampness, so one should promptly eat bitter foods to dry it. The lung governs autumn; the hand taiyin and yangming channels govern its treatment; its days are geng and xin. The lung suffers from qi rebelling upward, so one should promptly eat bitter foods to drain it. The kidney governs winter; the foot shaoyin and taiyang channels govern its treatment; its days are ren and gui. The kidney suffers from dryness, so one should promptly eat pungent foods to moisten it, opening the interstices, bringing forth fluids, and freeing the flow of qi.

When disease is in the liver, it resolves in summer; if it does not resolve in summer, it worsens in autumn; if it does not cause death in autumn, it stabilizes in winter and flares up again in spring. The patient should avoid exposure to wind. In liver disease, recovery occurs on the days bing and ding; if not resolved on bing and ding, it worsens on geng and xin; if it does not cause death on geng and xin, it stabilizes on ren and gui, and flares up again on jia and yi. In liver disease, the patient is clearer at dawn, worsens in the late afternoon, and is calm at midnight. The liver desires to disperse; one should promptly eat pungent foods to disperse it — use pungent flavor to supplement it and sour flavor to drain it.

When disease is in the heart, it resolves in long summer; if it does not resolve in long summer, it worsens in winter; if it does not cause death in winter, it stabilizes in spring and flares up again in summer. The patient should avoid warm food and hot clothing. In heart disease, recovery occurs on wu and ji; if not resolved on wu and ji, it worsens on ren and gui; if it does not cause death on ren and gui, it stabilizes on jia and yi, and flares up again on bing and ding. In heart disease, the patient is clearer at midday, worsens at midnight, and is calm at dawn. The heart desires softness; one should promptly eat salty foods to soften it — use salty flavor to supplement it and sweet flavor to drain it.

When disease is in the spleen, it resolves in autumn; if it does not resolve in autumn, it worsens in spring; if it does not cause death in spring, it stabilizes in summer and flares up again in long summer. The patient should avoid warm food, overeating, damp ground, and wet clothing. In spleen disease, recovery occurs on geng and xin; if not resolved on geng and xin, it worsens on jia and yi; if it does not cause death on jia and yi, it stabilizes on bing and ding, and flares up again on wu and ji. In spleen disease, the patient is clearer in the early afternoon, worsens at sunrise, and is calm in the late afternoon. The spleen desires relaxation; one should promptly eat sweet foods to relax it — use bitter flavor to drain it and sweet flavor to supplement it.

When disease is in the lung, it resolves in winter; if it does not resolve in winter, it worsens in summer; if it does not cause death in summer, it stabilizes in long summer and flares up again in autumn. The patient should avoid cold food, cold drink, and cold clothing. In lung disease, recovery occurs on ren and gui; if not resolved on ren and gui, it worsens on bing and ding; if it does not cause death on bing and ding, it stabilizes on wu and ji, and flares up again on geng and xin. In lung disease, the patient is clearer in the late afternoon, worsens at midday, and is calm at midnight. The lung desires constriction; one should promptly eat sour foods to constrict it — use sour flavor to supplement it and pungent flavor to drain it.

When disease is in the kidney, it resolves in spring; if it does not resolve in spring, it worsens in long summer; if it does not cause death in long summer, it stabilizes in autumn and flares up again in winter. The patient should avoid searing heat, hot food, and warm, overheated clothing. In kidney disease, recovery occurs on jia and yi; if not resolved on jia and yi, it worsens on wu and ji; if it does not cause death on wu and ji, it stabilizes on geng and xin, and flares up again on ren and gui. In kidney disease, the patient is clearer at midnight, worsens at the turning points of the four seasons, and is calm in the late afternoon. The kidney desires firmness; one should promptly eat bitter foods to firm it — use bitter flavor to supplement it and salty flavor to drain it.

When pathogenic qi lodges in the body, it advances by way of the phase that overcomes the affected organ's phase; the disease resolves when it reaches the phase that the organ generates; it worsens when it reaches the phase that overcomes the organ; it stabilizes when it reaches the phase that generates the organ; and it flares up again when it reaches the organ's own proper phase-position. One must first determine the pulses of the five viscera before one can speak of the timing of remission and exacerbation, and the periods of death and life.

In liver disease, there is pain under both flanks pulling into the lower abdomen, causing the person to be prone to anger. In deficiency, the eyes are dim and see nothing, the ears hear nothing, and the person is prone to fear, as if someone were about to seize him. Select the jueyin and shaoyang channels. When the qi rebels upward, there is headache, deafness, impaired hearing, and swelling of the cheeks; bleed the channel.

In heart disease, there is pain in the chest, a bracing fullness in the flanks, pain under the flanks, pain in the chest, back, and between the shoulder blades, and pain along the inner arms. In deficiency, the chest and abdomen become distended, with pain pulling between the flanks and the lower back. Select the shaoyin and taiyang channels, and bleed under the tongue. For its complicated transformations, needle and bleed the Weizhong (Bend Center) point.

In spleen disease, the body feels heavy, the muscles tend toward wasting, the feet cannot be controlled, walking is often accompanied by spasms, and there is pain in the soles of the feet. In deficiency, the abdomen is distended and there is intestinal rumbling, with undigested food in loose stools. Select the taiyin, yangming, and shaoyin channels and bleed them.

In lung disease, there is panting and coughing with rebellious qi, pain in the shoulders and back, sweating, and pain throughout the buttocks, genitals, thighs, knees, hips, calves, shins, and feet. In deficiency, there is shortness of breath such that one cannot complete an exhalation, deafness, and dryness of the throat. Select the taiyin channel, the points outside the foot taiyin channel, and the points inside the jueyin channel, and bleed them.

In kidney disease, the abdomen is distended and the shins are swollen, with panting, coughing, and heaviness of the body; night sweats occur during sleep, and there is aversion to wind. In deficiency, there is pain in the chest, pain in the upper and lower abdomen, cold reversal of the limbs, and a despondent mood. Select the shaoyin and taiyang channels and bleed them.

The liver's color is blue-green; it is proper to eat sweet foods — polished rice, beef, jujubes, and mallow are all sweet. The heart's color is red; it is proper to eat sour foods — red beans, dog meat, plums, and chives are all sour. The lung's color is white; it is proper to eat bitter foods — wheat, mutton, apricots, and scallion-leek are all bitter. The spleen's color is yellow; it is proper to eat salty foods — soybeans, pork, chestnuts, and bean leaves are all salty. The kidney's color is black; it is proper to eat pungent foods — yellow millet, chicken, peaches, and scallions are all pungent. Pungent flavor disperses, sour flavor constricts, sweet flavor relaxes, bitter flavor firms, and salty flavor softens. Potent drugs attack pathogenic qi; the five grains provide nourishment; the five fruits give assistance; the five kinds of meat provide benefit; the five vegetables supply fullness. When qi and flavor are properly combined and taken, they supplement essence and boost qi. Of these five flavors — pungent, sour, sweet, bitter, and salty — each has its own benefit: some disperse, some constrict; some relax, some tighten; some firm, some soften. According to the four seasons and the five viscera, disease is treated according to whichever of the five flavors is appropriate.
Where the flavors enter: sour enters the liver, pungent enters the lung, bitter enters the heart, salty enters the kidney, and sweet enters the spleen. This is called the five entries.

Where qi disorder causes disease: in the heart it causes belching, in the lung it causes coughing, in the liver it causes excessive talking, in the spleen it causes swallowing reflux, in the kidney it causes yawning and sneezing. In the stomach it causes rebellious qi, retching, and fear; in the large and small intestines it causes diarrhea; overflow in the lower burner causes water swelling; inhibited function of the bladder causes dribbling urinary blockage, and loss of control causes incontinence; in the gallbladder it causes anger. This is called the five diseases of qi.

Where essence-qi concentrates: when essence-qi concentrates in the heart, there is joy; when it concentrates in the lung, there is sorrow; when it concentrates in the liver, there is worry; when it concentrates in the spleen, there is apprehension; when it concentrates in the kidney, there is fear. This is called the five concentrations — they occur when an organ is deficient and qi concentrates within it.
What the viscera dislike: the heart dislikes heat, the lung dislikes cold, the liver dislikes wind, the spleen dislikes dampness, and the kidney dislikes dryness. This is called the five aversions.

What the viscera transform into fluids: the heart produces sweat, the lung produces nasal mucus, the liver produces tears, the spleen produces thin saliva, and the kidney produces thick spittle. This is called the five fluids.

Flavors to be avoided: pungent flavor moves to the qi, so in qi disease one should not eat much pungent food; salty flavor moves to the blood, so in blood disease one should not eat much salty food; bitter flavor moves to the bones, so in bone disease one should not eat much bitter food; sweet flavor moves to the flesh, so in flesh disease one should not eat much sweet food; sour flavor moves to the sinews, so in sinew disease one should not eat much sour food. This is called the five prohibitions — one must not overeat these.

Where diseases arise: yin disease arises in the bones, yang disease arises in the blood, yin disease arises in the flesh, yang disease arises in winter, yin disease arises in summer. This is called the five arisings.

Where pathogenic qi causes disorder: when pathogenic qi enters the yang, it causes mania; when it enters the yin, it causes impediment (bi-syndrome); when it clashes with the yang, it causes disease of the vertex; when it clashes with the yin, it causes muteness; when yang pathogenic qi enters the yin, the person becomes quiet; when yin pathogenic qi emerges into the yang, the person becomes angry. This is called the five disorders.

Where pathogenic pulses appear: in spring, finding the autumn pulse; in summer, finding the winter pulse; in long summer, finding the spring pulse; in autumn, finding the summer pulse; in winter, finding the long-summer pulse — these are called yin emerging into yang. The disease manifesting this way tends toward anger and is untreatable. This is called the five pathogenic pulses; all share the same fate — death, and cannot be treated.

What the viscera store: the heart stores the spirit, the lung stores the corporeal soul (po), the liver stores the ethereal soul (hun), the spleen stores intention, and the kidney stores will. This is called what the five viscera store.

What the viscera govern: the heart governs the vessels, the lung governs the skin, the liver governs the sinews, the spleen governs the flesh, and the kidney governs the bones. This is called the five governings.

What overexertion injures: prolonged looking injures the blood, prolonged lying injures the qi, prolonged sitting injures the flesh, prolonged standing injures the bones, prolonged walking injures the sinews. This is called the injuries of the five kinds of overexertion.

The pulse images corresponding to the viscera: the liver's pulse is string-like, the heart's pulse is hook-like, the spleen's pulse is intermittent, the lung's pulse is feather-like, and the kidney's pulse is stone-like. This is called the pulses of the five viscera.

title_en: Blood and Qi, Body and Mind
paragraphs_en: As for the constant proportions in man: the taiyang channels normally have abundant blood and little qi; the shaoyang channels normally have little blood and abundant qi; the yangming channels normally have abundant qi and abundant blood; the shaoyin channels normally have little blood and abundant qi; the jueyin channels normally have abundant blood and little qi; the taiyin channels normally have abundant qi and little blood. These are the constant proportions ordained by heaven. The foot taiyang and shaoyin are exterior-interior pairs, the shaoyang and jueyin are exterior-interior pairs, and the yangming and taiyin are exterior-interior pairs — these are the yin and yang of the foot channels. The hand taiyang and shaoyin are exterior-interior pairs, the shaoyang and the pericardium (heart-ruler) are exterior-interior pairs, and the yangming and taiyin are exterior-interior pairs — these are the yin and yang of the hand channels. Now, knowing what afflicts the hand and foot yin and yang channels, in treating disease one must first remove excess blood, and then remove what afflicts the patient, attending to what the qi desires, and only then drain what is in excess and supplement what is deficient.

To find the back transport (shu) points: first measure the distance between the two nipples, and fold the measuring cord in half at the middle point. Then take another stalk and measure off half of that folded length, so that the two ends of this shorter piece, when joined at right angles with the first, form a set-square. Then raise it to measure along the back, positioning one corner at the top, level with the Da Zhui point on the spine, with the other two corners below. The point at the lower corner is the lung's transport point. Measuring one further increment down is the heart's transport point. Measuring one further increment down, the left corner is the liver's transport point and the right corner is the spleen's transport point. Measuring one further increment down is the kidney's transport point. These are called the transport points of the five viscera, the measurements for moxibustion and needling.

When the body is at ease but the mind is troubled, disease arises in the vessels; treat it with moxibustion and needling. When the body is at ease and the mind is at ease, disease arises in the flesh; treat it with needles and stone probes. When the body is troubled but the mind is at ease, disease arises in the sinews; treat it with hot compresses and guided stretching exercises. When the body is troubled and the mind is troubled, disease arises in the throat; treat it with the hundred medicines. When the body is repeatedly startled and frightened, the channels and network vessels become blocked, and disease arises as numbness; treat it with massage and medicated wine. These are called the five combinations of body and mind.

In needling the yangming channels, bleed and let out qi. In needling the taiyang channels, bleed but avoid letting out qi. In needling the shaoyang channels, let out qi but avoid bleeding. In needling the taiyin channels, let out qi but avoid bleeding. In needling the shaoyin channels, let out qi but avoid bleeding. In needling the jueyin channels, bleed but avoid letting out qi.

title_en: Discourse on Treasuring Life and Preserving the Bodily Form
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Heaven covers and earth carries all things; the ten thousand things are all complete within this cosmos, yet nothing is more precious than man. Man is born from the qi of heaven and earth, and comes to maturity according to the pattern of the four seasons. Rulers and commoners alike all wish to preserve their bodily form intact, yet when disease afflicts the body, none understand its true nature; it lingers and spreads, growing deeper day by day, until it lodges in the bone marrow. This troubles my heart. I wish to use needling to remove such disease — how should this be done? Qibo replied: When salt has a salty flavor, its qi will cause the vessel containing it to seep moisture; when a bowstring is about to snap, its sound becomes hoarse and broken; when a tree is diseased, its leaves wither; when a person's disease is deep, his voice becomes broken and retching. When a person shows these three signs, this is called a ruined organ-system — potent drugs cannot treat it, and short needles cannot draw it out. In all these cases, the skin is severed and the flesh damaged, and blood and qi struggle and turn dark.

Huangdi said: I am troubled thinking of their suffering; my heart is thrown into confusion and distress over it, for the disease is only worsened and cannot be undone. When the common people hear of this, they take the physician for a maiming brigand. What is to be done? Qibo said: Man is born of the earth and his life is suspended from heaven; when the qi of heaven and earth combine, this is called man. One who can respond to the four seasons has heaven and earth as father and mother; one who understands the ten thousand things is called the Son of Heaven. Heaven has yin and yang; man has the twelve joints. Heaven has cold and heat; man has deficiency and excess. One who can trace through the transformations of yin and yang in heaven and earth will not lose step with the four seasons; one who understands the principles of the twelve joints cannot be deceived even by sage wisdom. One who can retain understanding of the changes of the eight movements of qi, by which the five phases' ascendancies alternately establish themselves, and who can penetrate the numerical patterns of deficiency and excess, moves alone and acts alone — even the faintest sigh or moan is perceived, and the finest hair of autumn is visible to his eye.

Huangdi said: Man's life has bodily form and cannot be separated from yin and yang. Heaven and earth combine their qi and differentiate into the nine regions, and divide into the four seasons; months have their short and long durations, days have their short and long lengths; the ten thousand things arrive together in such abundance that they cannot be fully measured, manifesting as deficiency and excess, sighing and moaning. May I ask about the method of treating this? Qibo said: Wood, meeting metal, is felled; fire, meeting water, is extinguished; earth, meeting wood, is penetrated; metal, meeting fire, is notched; water, meeting earth, is cut off. All the ten thousand things are thus, and this cannot be exhausted. Therefore there are five things about needling that hang displayed before all under heaven, as common as the leftover food shared among the common people, yet none understand them. The first is called governing the spirit; the second is knowing how to nourish the body; the third is knowing the true efficacy of medicinal substances; the fourth is knowing how to fashion stone probes of the proper size; the fifth is knowing how to diagnose the blood and qi of the bowels and viscera. When these five methods are all established, each has its priority. Now, in the needling practiced in this degenerate age, physicians merely supplement what is deficient and drain what is full — this is all that the multitude of practitioners commonly know. But as for modeling oneself on heaven and taking earth as one's law, moving in accord with what responds — true skill harmonizes as an echo answers a sound, and follows as a shadow follows a form; the Way has no ghosts or spirits in it, and the skilled physician comes and goes alone by his own understanding.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear about the Way of this. Qibo said: In all true needling, one must first govern the spirit. Once the state of the five viscera is determined and the nine indicators are complete, only then does one focus the mind on the needle — not distracted by the multitude of other pulses, not disturbed by hearing of the multitude of ill omens, exterior and interior brought into accord, without letting outward appearance take precedence. One may then observe the coming and going of qi, and only then apply the needle to the person. In man there is deficiency and excess: in the five deficiencies, do not approach carelessly; in the five excesses, do not hold back. When the moment for insertion arrives, there is no room even for the width of a hair between readiness and action. The hand moves as if intent on urgent work, the needle gleaming and even in its motion; with a calm mind, observe the meaning of the pulse, and watch for the changes indicating the proper moment. This is called the imperceptible and obscure — none can know its form. One sees it as crows gathering in a flock, one sees it swarming densely, one then sees it take flight, yet knows not what it is; at rest it is like a crossbow drawn and held level, in motion like the release of its trigger. Huangdi said: What is meant by deficiency, and what is meant by excess? Qibo said: In needling deficiency, one waits for the qi to become replete; in needling excess, one waits for it to become drained. Once the channel qi has arrived, guard it carefully and do not lose it. The choice of depth and shallowness lies in the intent of the physician; whether the site is far or near, the focus should be as one, undivided. Approach it as if overlooking a deep abyss; hold the hand as if grasping a tiger; let the spirit not be distracted by the multitude of other things.

title_en: Discourse on the Eight Correct Periods and Spirit Illumination
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: In the practice of using needles, there must be rules and standards. What are these rules and standards? Qibo replied: One models on heaven and takes law from earth, according with the light of heaven — the sun, moon, and stars. Huangdi said: I wish to hear the whole of it. Qibo said: In the method of needling, one must always observe the qi of the sun, moon, stars, the four seasons, and the eight correct periods; only when the qi is settled should one needle. Therefore, when the weather is warm and the sun bright, people's blood becomes fluid and moist, and the defensive qi floats near the surface, so blood is easily drained and qi easily moves; when the weather is cold and the sky overcast, people's blood congeals and stagnates, and the defensive qi sinks. When the moon begins to wax, blood and qi begin to grow refined and the defensive qi begins to circulate; when the moon's disk is full, blood and qi become replete and the flesh becomes firm; when the moon's disk is empty, the flesh diminishes, the channels and network vessels become empty, the defensive qi departs, and the bodily form stands alone and unsupported. Therefore one adjusts blood and qi in accordance with the timing of heaven. For this reason, do not needle when the weather is cold, and do not hesitate to needle when the weather is warm. Do not drain when the moon is waxing; do not supplement when the moon is full; do not treat when the moon's disk is empty — this is called obtaining the proper time and regulating accordingly. Following the sequence of heaven and the times of abundance and depletion, one tracks the movement of light to fix one's position, and stands correctly to await the right moment. Therefore, to drain when the sun and moon are newly waxing is called emptying the viscera; to supplement when the moon is full causes blood and qi to overflow in excess, with stagnant blood lodging in the network vessels — this is called doubled excess; to treat when the moon's disk is empty is called disordering the channels. In such cases yin and yang become interlaced and confused, true qi and pathogenic qi cannot be distinguished, pathogenic qi sinks in and lingers, the exterior becomes deficient and the interior disordered, and excessive pathogenic influences then arise.

Huangdi said: What do the stars and the eight correct periods indicate? Qibo said: The stars are what regulate the movements of the sun and moon. The eight correct periods are what indicate the depleting pathogenic influence of the eight winds, which arrive at their proper times. The four seasons are what divide where the qi of spring, autumn, winter, and summer resides, so that one may regulate accordingly with the season; one must recognize the depleting pathogenic winds of the eight correct periods and avoid exposure to them. When bodily deficiency meets with heaven's deficiency, the two deficiencies resonate with each other, and this pathogenic qi penetrates to the bone; entering, it damages the five viscera. But if the practitioner watches for it and treats it in time, it cannot cause harm. Therefore it is said: the prohibitions of heaven must not go unknown.

Huangdi said: Good. I have now heard about modeling on the stars; I wish to hear about modeling on the ancients. Qibo said: To model on the ancients is first to know the classics of needling. To verify one's practice in the present day is first to know the cold and warmth of the days and the waxing and waning of the moon, so as to observe whether qi floats or sinks, and regulate it in the body, observing that this immediately proves effective. As for observing what is obscure and unseen — this refers to the fact that the bodily form, qi, and nutritive and defensive qi do not show themselves outwardly, yet the skilled practitioner alone knows them: by the cold and warmth of the day, the waning and waxing of the moon, and the floating and sinking of qi through the four seasons, examined together and cross-checked, he regulates accordingly. The skilled practitioner always perceives this beforehand, though it does not show outwardly — hence it is called observing what is obscure and unseen. One who penetrates the inexhaustible may transmit this knowledge to later generations; this is precisely wherein the skilled practitioner differs from others, yet since it does not show outwardly, others altogether cannot perceive it. Looked at, it has no form; tasted, it has no flavor — hence it is called the obscure and unseen, as if divine, as if numinous. As for depleting pathogenic qi — this is the depleting pathogenic qi of the eight correct periods. As for upright, ordinary pathogenic influence — when the body exerts effort, sweat is emitted, and the interstices open, and one then meets an ordinary depleting wind, it strikes the person only lightly, so that none can know its true nature nor see its form. The superior practitioner treats disease at its budding stage — he must first perceive the qi of the three sections and nine indicators, regulating fully before it has deteriorated, and thereby rescues the patient; hence he is called a superior practitioner. The inferior practitioner treats disease only after it has already formed, or after it has already broken down. Treating it after it has already formed means he does not know how the three sections and nine indicators have lost their proper coordination, and so the disease is allowed to progress to breakdown. Knowing where the disease lies means knowing how to diagnose the diseased pulse locations of the three sections and nine indicators and treat accordingly. Hence it is said: guard the gateways of the body — for none can know the pathogen's true nature, yet the skilled practitioner can perceive the pathogen's form.

Huangdi said: I have heard of supplementing and draining, but have not grasped their meaning. Qibo said: Draining must employ the 'square' method — 'square' meaning to act when the qi is just at its height, when the moon is just full, when the day is just warm, when the body is just settled, and when the breath is just inhaling — one inserts the needle then; then again one waits for the next inhalation to rotate the needle; then again one waits for the exhalation to slowly withdraw the needle. Hence it is said: draining must employ the 'square' timing, so that the pathogenic qi is made to move out. Supplementing must employ the 'round' method — 'round' meaning to move, and to move means to shift; the needling must strike the nutritive qi precisely, and the needle is then withdrawn upon inhalation. Thus 'round' and 'square' do not refer to the shape of the needle itself, but to the manner and timing of its use. Therefore, one who nourishes the spirit must know whether the body is fat or thin, and the abundance or decline of the nutritive and defensive qi and of blood and qi. Blood and qi are the person's spirit, and must not fail to be carefully nourished.

Huangdi said: How subtle and marvelous is this discourse! Uniting the human bodily form with yin and yang and the four seasons, the correspondences of deficiency and excess, and the timing of what is obscure and unseen — who but you, Master, could penetrate these things? Yet you have repeatedly spoken of form and spirit — what is meant by form, and what is meant by spirit? I wish to hear the whole of it. Qibo said: Let me speak of form. Form, ah, form — even when the eyes are dim and cannot see it directly, one asks about the patient's disease and seeks it in the channels: it becomes clearly present before one, yet when pressed for, it cannot be grasped, and its true nature remains unknown — hence it is called form. Huangdi said: What is meant by spirit? Qibo said: Let me speak of spirit. Spirit, ah, spirit — the ears do not hear it, but the eyes become clear and the heart opens, and the intention goes before; one comes to a sudden and solitary realization, which the mouth cannot put into words. All look upon it, yet the skilled practitioner alone perceives it; it is as if in dimness, and then suddenly and uniquely bright, like wind blowing away the clouds — hence it is called spirit. The three sections and nine indicators are its foundation; as for the discourses on the nine needles, they need not necessarily be retained.

title_en: The Divergence and Convergence of True and Pathogenic Qi
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard of the Nine Needles in nine chapters, and you, Master, multiplied this ninefold, into nine times nine — eighty-one chapters — and I have now thoroughly grasped their meaning. The classics speak of the waxing and waning of qi, its shifting to the left and right, of regulating below by means of above, regulating the right by means of the left, and of supplementing and draining the nutritive qi at the transport points according to excess and insufficiency — these I understand. But all of this concerns the shifting of nutritive and defensive qi, and how deficiency and excess arise from within — not pathogenic qi entering the channels from outside. I wish to hear about pathogenic qi being present in the channels: how does it afflict a person, and how is it treated? Qibo replied: When the sages established their measures and numbers, these necessarily corresponded to heaven and earth. Thus heaven has its lodge-degrees of the stars, earth has its rivers, and man has his channels and vessels. When heaven and earth are warm and mild, the rivers are calm and still; when heaven is cold and earth is frozen, the rivers congeal and stagnate; when heaven is hot and earth is scorching, the rivers boil and overflow; when a sudden violent wind arises, the rivers surge in waves and swell into mounds. So too, when pathogenic qi enters the vessels: in cold, the blood congeals and stagnates; in heat, the qi becomes fluid and moist; depleting pathogenic qi thereby enters and lodges in the channels, just as the rivers are affected by wind. The pulsing vessels of the channels, when pathogenic qi arrives, likewise at times swell into mounds; normally, qi travels through the vessels smoothly and continuously, but when it reaches the cunkou pulse under the examining hand, it is at times large and at times small — when large, pathogenic qi has arrived; when small, the pulse is calm. Its movement has no constant location; whether in yin or yang channels, it cannot be measured by fixed rule. One must trace and examine it through the three sections and nine indicators, and when one suddenly encounters it, one must block its path early. Insert the needle upon inhalation, so as not to let it clash with the flow of qi; hold it still and retain it for a long while, so as not to let the pathogenic qi spread; upon the next inhalation, rotate the needle, aiming to obtain the arrival of qi; wait for the exhalation and then withdraw the needle, removing it only once the exhalation is complete, so that the great pathogenic qi is entirely expelled — this is called draining. Huangdi said: How does one supplement what is insufficient? Qibo said: One must first press and trace along the vessel with the palm, pinch and disperse any obstruction, push and press upon it, flick it to make it swell up, scratch the skin and press the needle down, and open a path to insert it. Draw open its gateway from outside so as to close in its spirit within; insert the needle only upon completion of the exhalation, hold it still and retain it for a long while, aiming to obtain the arrival of qi — waiting as one would await something precious, unmindful of the lateness of the day, until the qi arrives. Then attend to it and guard it carefully; wait for the inhalation and then withdraw the needle, so that the qi cannot escape but remains each in its place; press the gateway closed, so that the spirit-qi is preserved and the great qi is retained and held in place — this is called supplementing.

Huangdi said: How does one watch for the arrival of qi? Qibo said: When pathogenic qi leaves the network vessels and enters the channels, it lodges within the blood vessels; before its coldness or warmth has settled with the body, it is like a surging wave rising up — coming at times, going at times, and thus not constantly present. Hence it is said: just as it arrives, one must press and stop it, stop it and then draw it out, but must not meet it head-on and drain it prematurely. True qi is the channel qi; if the channel qi is already quite deficient, one must not attack it as pathogenic qi arrives — this is the meaning of 'one must not meet it upon its arrival.' Hence it is said: if one watches for pathogenic qi without due care, and the great pathogenic qi has already passed, then to drain at that point would cause the true qi to be lost; once lost, it cannot be restored, and the pathogenic qi will return again, so that the disease accumulates further — hence it is said: 'one cannot pursue it after it has departed' — this is the meaning of that. As for the timing being so precise that it cannot be off even by the breadth of a hair: one must wait for the moment the pathogenic qi arrives and then release the needle to drain it; if one acts too early or too late, the blood and qi will already be exhausted, and the disease cannot then be brought down. Hence it is said: knowing when it can be drawn out is like releasing a trigger; not knowing how to draw it out is like knocking on a wooden post that will not sound. Hence it is said: one who knows the way of the critical moment will not be off even by the breadth of a hair; one who does not know the critical moment will knock and get no response — this is the meaning of that. Huangdi said: How is supplementing and draining done in this case? Qibo said: This is a matter of attacking pathogenic qi — one must swiftly let it out so as to remove the pooled blood and restore the true qi. When the pathogenic qi is newly lodged, it drifts about without a fixed location: pushed, it moves forward; pulled, it stops. Needle against its direction of movement — this warms the blood. Needle to let out its blood, and the disease will be resolved at once.

Huangdi said: Good. But when true qi and pathogenic qi have already merged, and no wave or mound rises — how does one examine for it then? Qibo said: Carefully press and trace along the three sections and nine indicators for abundance and deficiency, and regulate accordingly; observe where left and right, above and below, fail to correspond or are diminished relative to each other; carefully determine the diseased viscus and gauge the critical time accordingly. One who does not understand the three sections cannot distinguish yin from yang, nor differentiate heaven from earth. Among the nine indicators, the earth positions examine the earth, the heaven positions examine the heaven, and the man positions examine the man; one regulates them at the central organs, so as to fix the three sections. Hence it is said: if one needles without knowing the locations of the diseased pulses of the three sections and nine indicators, then even though a great excess arrives, the practitioner cannot prevent it. To punish where there is no fault is called great confusion — on the contrary it throws the great channels into disorder, so that the true qi cannot be restored; such a practitioner treats excess as deficiency and takes pathogenic qi for true qi; his use of the needle is without proper principle, and instead becomes a brigand against qi, robbing the person of their upright qi; he treats compliance as rebellion, so that nutritive and defensive qi scatter into disorder and true qi is already lost, while pathogenic qi alone remains lodged within — this cuts short the person's long life and brings upon him calamity from heaven. Not knowing the three sections and nine indicators, such a practitioner cannot help patients endure long. Because he does not know how to correlate treatment with the four seasons and the five phases, and misapplies the mutual-overcoming relations among the phases, he releases the pathogenic and attacks the upright, thereby cutting short the person's long life. When pathogenic qi is newly arrived as a guest in the body, it has as yet no fixed location: pushed, it moves forward; pulled, it stops. Meet it directly and drain it, and the disease will be resolved at once.
